name: Disk Management with LVM
description: Manage logical volumes, extend filesystems, and monitor disk health using LVM and standard disk utilities.

steps.title: View physical volumes
steps.description: List all physical volumes in the LVM system.

steps.title: View volume groups and free space
steps.description: Check volume groups and how much space is available for new logical volumes.

steps.title: List logical volumes with details
steps.description: View all logical volumes and their sizes.

steps.title: Extend a logical volume and resize the filesystem
steps.description: Add more space to an existing logical volume and grow the filesystem to use it.
steps.warning: For XFS filesystems, use 'xfs_growfs /mount/point' instead of resize2fs.
steps.tip: Use -l +100%FREE to use all remaining free space in the volume group.

steps.title: Check filesystem health
steps.description: Verify filesystem integrity. Must be run on unmounted or read-only filesystems.
steps.warning: Never run fsck on a mounted read-write filesystem. Use -n for a read-only check.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Disk Management Overview
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Disk Usage ==="
df -h | grep -v tmpfs

echo ""
echo "=== Block Devices ==="
lsblk -f

echo ""
echo "=== LVM Status ==="
echo "--- Physical Volumes ---"
sudo pvs 2>/dev/null || echo "No PVs or LVM not installed"

echo ""
echo "--- Volume Groups ---"
sudo vgs 2>/dev/null || echo "No VGs"

echo ""
echo "--- Logical Volumes ---"
sudo lvs 2>/dev/null || echo "No LVs"

echo ""
echo "=== Disk Health ==="
for disk in $(lsblk -dn -o NAME | grep -E '^sd|^nvme'); do
  echo "--- /dev/$disk ---"
  sudo smartctl -H "/dev/$disk" 2>/dev/null | grep -i 'health\|result' || echo "SMART not available"
done

faq.question: Can I extend a logical volume while the filesystem is mounted?
faq.answer: Yes, for ext4 and XFS filesystems. lvextend and resize2fs/xfs_growfs support online resizing. No unmount is needed for growing (but shrinking ext4 requires unmounting).

faq.question: How do I add a new disk to an existing volume group?
faq.answer: Create a physical volume with 'pvcreate /dev/sdb', then extend the volume group with 'vgextend vg0 /dev/sdb'. The new space is immediately available for lvextend.
